titel: A new system of criminal penalties.
html:
In the proposal, the government presents several suggestions aimed at creating a more just penal system where punishments better reflect the severity of the crime, the imposed sentences appear more reasonable and fair, the perspective of victims is given greater weight, and societal safety is improved. The government proposes, among other things, the following:

  • A system of conditional imprisonment will be introduced. The presumption against imprisonment will be removed, and the penalties of suspended sentence and supervised release will be phased out.
  • Sentencing should be carried out in a more nuanced manner.
  • In cases of multiple offenses, subsequent crimes should have a greater impact on sentencing. Sentencing should be based on the total severity of the crimes for which the defendant is convicted.
  • Certain mitigating factors, known as "equity considerations," will be removed.

The proposed legislative changes are to come into effect on the date determined by the government.

Proposal for Parliamentary Decision

Prop. 2025/26:297

Government Proposal:

  1. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed law regarding the enforcement of suspended sentences.
  2. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Penal Code.
  3. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 2025:1415, which amends the Penal Code.
  4. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 2025:1416, which amends the Penal Code.
  5. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Code of Judicial Procedure.
  6. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Social Security Code.
  7. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 1930:173 regarding the calculation of legally mandated time.
  8. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 1946:804, introducing the new Code of Judicial Procedure.
  9. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 1957:668 regarding extradition for criminal offenses.
  10. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 1964:167 with special provisions concerning young offenders.
  11. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Passport Act (1978:302).
  12. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 1978:801 regarding international cooperation in criminal justice within a free society.
  13. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Fines Enforcement Act (1979:189).
  14. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 1988:688 regarding contact bans.
  15. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 1988:870 regarding care for drug abusers in certain cases.
  16. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 1991:1137 regarding psychiatric evaluations.
  17. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 1991:2041 regarding special investigations in criminal cases, etc.
  18. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 1998:620 regarding the criminal record register.
  19. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 1998:714 regarding compensation for detentions and other coercive measures.
  20. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Aliens Act (2005:716).
  21. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Prison Act (2010:610).
  22. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Detention Act (2010:611).
  23. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 2015:96 regarding the recognition and enforcement of deprivation of liberty within the European Union.
  24. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 2015:650 regarding the recognition and enforcement of probationary sentences within the European Union.
  25. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 2018:1250 regarding the crediting of time served for deprivation of liberty.
  26. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Sentence Length Act (2018:1251).
  27. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 2018:1699 regarding the processing of personal data by the criminal justice system within the scope of the Criminal Data Act.
  28. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 2020:616 regarding the enforcement of youth supervision.
  29. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 2022:482 regarding electronic communication.
  30. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 2024:326 regarding secret coercive measures intended to enforce deprivation of liberty sentences.
  31. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 2025:520 regarding international enforcement in criminal cases.
  32. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 2025:1053 regarding the enforcement of prison sentences with electronic monitoring.
  33. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to Act 2026:786 regarding Nordic enforcement in criminal cases.
tldr: The government wants to change the rules for how sentences are determined and carried out in order to make the justice system more fair. This includes new regulations regarding imprisonment, stricter penalties for individuals who commit multiple offenses, and improved protection for society.
lattlast:
The government has proposed changes to the laws governing how individuals are convicted and sentenced for crimes. The goal is to ensure that punishments better reflect the severity of the offenses, making them more just for both victims and society.

A key component of the proposal is the introduction of a system of conditional imprisonment. This involves modifying how courts choose between different types of penalties. The proposal also aims to make it easier to impose harsher sentences on individuals who commit multiple crimes by aggregating all offenses when determining prison time.

The proposal also includes the removal of certain regulations that were previously used to reduce sentences. The government also intends to improve rules related to, among other things, contact bans, care for substance abusers, and how individuals are monitored whether they are incarcerated or serving a sentence in the community. All changes will come into effect when the government determines.
